metadata.title: can you commit and push cha...

content.data: The current directory isn't a git repository. If you have a specific project path in mind, let me know and I can check there instead.
I'm already working in the current directory. What would you like me to do here?

There are quite a few changes. Before I commit, a couple of questions:

1. What commit message would you like?
2. Should I include the untracked files (new `.qmd` notes files, `.gitignore`, images, etc.) or just commit the modified/deleted files?
Just the modified/deleted files then. What commit message would you like?
Committed and pushed to `main` on GitHub (29 files changed).
Already done — it was pushed to GitHub in the last step (`https://github.com/wsneddon/ss-lessons-from-John`).
You're welcome! Good luck with lesson 03. 😊
Found 10 files. Shall I delete all of them?

Done, all 10 notes.qmd files deleted.

As for generating PDFs from your Quarto content — since you're already using Quarto, you can add PDF as an output format in your `_quarto.yml`:

```yaml
format:
  html: ...
  pdf: default
```

Or render a single file to PDF with:
```bash
quarto render 01-Intro/introduction.qmd --to pdf
```

This requires a LaTeX installation (e.g. TinyTeX: `quarto install tinytex`). Want me to set that up in your project?

All done:

- TinyTeX installed
- `_quarto.yml` updated to add `pdf: default` as an output format and notes files removed from the render list
- Changes committed and pushed

Now when you run `quarto render`, it will produce both HTML and PDF for each lesson. To render a single file: `quarto render 01-Intro/introduction.qmd`.

Those are just embedded PDFs inside your lesson content, not rendered output. The lessons haven't been rendered to PDF yet.

To generate them, run:
```bash
quarto render
```

Quarto will place each PDF alongside its `.qmd` file — e.g., `01-Intro/introduction.pdf`, `02 - Jesus as God/jesus-as-god.pdf`, etc.

No new PDFs were created. The render may have failed or only produced HTML. Can you check the terminal output from the render — were there any errors?

The issue is likely that your `.qmd` files use `revealjs` format (slides), and PDF output from revealjs requires a different approach than standard PDF. Quarto can't directly render revealjs to PDF via LaTeX — it would need to render each file individually with `--to pdf` to get a document-style PDF instead.

Want me to check one of your `.qmd` files to see what format they're using?

As suspected — the files are revealjs (slide) format, not document format. That's why PDF rendering didn't work; LaTeX can't render revealjs slides.

You have two options:

1. **Print-to-PDF from browser** — open the HTML slide in a browser, add `?print-pdf` to the URL, then print to PDF. This is the standard revealjs approach and preserves the slide layout.

2. **Create a separate document-format `.qmd`** for each lesson that renders to PDF — essentially a plain document version of the same content.

Given your content uses collapsible `<details>` HTML elements, option 1 (browser print-to-PDF) is probably the most practical. Would you like to go that route, or would you prefer a separate document format for sharing?

Yes — `<details>` elements only print what's currently open. Collapsed sections would appear as just the summary line in the PDF.

The cleanest solution for sharing with students would be a separate document-format `.qmd` that renders to HTML (or PDF) with all content visible and no collapsing. You'd essentially have:

- `introduction.qmd` → revealjs slides (for teaching)
- `introduction-handout.qmd` → plain document (for students)

The handout could reuse the same content but formatted as a simple readable document. Want me to create one for the Introduction lesson as a template you could follow for the others?

PDF generated successfully (23K). Now a single `quarto render` will build all 9 handout PDFs alongside your slides. The handouts are placed next to each lesson's `.qmd` — e.g., `01-Intro/introduction-handout.pdf`.

Want me to open the PDF so you can check the formatting, or commit everything now?
It should be open in Preview now. Take a look and let me know if the formatting needs any adjustments before we render all the lessons and commit.
PDF re-opened — text should now wrap properly. How does it look?
PDF is open — each item should now be on its own line as a proper bulleted list. How does it look?
PDF is open — wrapping should be fixed now. How does it look?
PDF is open — no more bullet markers, and indentation via em-spaces which won't trigger code blocks. How does it look now?
PDF is open — em-dashes and special characters should now render correctly with XeLaTeX + Arial font. How does it look?

Those `—` dashes are actually in the source content itself (not encoding issues) — they're part of the text. That's how your lesson content is written. Nothing to fix there — that's intentional content.

Is the rendering/formatting otherwise looking good now? The em-spaces for indentation, text wrapping, no bullet markers?
PDF is open — leading `—` should now show as `•` bullet points. How does it look?
48 links found and preserved. PDF is open — links should now appear as clickable `[text](url)` style in the PDF. How does it look?
PDF is open — links should now show cleanly as `[link text](url)` without duplication. How does it look?
PDF is open — a., b., and c. should now all have their links. How does it look?
Fixed. Want me to commit all the changes (handout files, script, and the source fix) now?

The Python files exist on disk in your project directory:

- `/Users/billsneddon/Data/personal/Church/SS/John/make_handouts.py`
- `/Users/billsneddon/Data/personal/Church/SS/John/make_signin.py` (was already there)

They'll persist between sessions as long as they're on disk. However they're not committed to git yet, so if you ever clone fresh or lose the files they'd be gone. Want me to commit everything now to make sure it's all saved to GitHub?

All committed and pushed. Everything is now saved to GitHub:
- `make_handouts.py` — the handout generator script
- All 9 `*-handout.qmd` files
- Updated `_quarto.yml` and `.gitignore`
- Fixed `introduction.qmd`

To regenerate handouts in a future session, just run `python3 make_handouts.py` from the project directory.

I don't retain memory between sessions — each new chat starts fresh. But the good news is everything you need is already in the repo, so the procedure is simple:

**When you add a new lesson:**
1. Create your lesson `.qmd` (e.g. `09 - New Lesson/new-lesson.qmd`)
2. Add it to the `render:` list in `_quarto.yml`
3. Add it to the `LESSONS` list in `make_handouts.py`
4. Run `python3 make_handouts.py` — generates the handout `.qmd`
5. Run `quarto render` — builds all slides + handout PDFs

**To remind me in a future session**, just say something like:
> "I'm working in `/Users/billsneddon/Data/personal/Church/SS/John` — check `make_handouts.py` and `_quarto.yml` to understand the project setup."

That's all I need — the code is self-documenting enough that I can pick up from there.

Yes, both are available! Here's what you can do:

**Save this session:**
```
/chat save ~/john-project-session.json
```

**Resume next time** — either start Kiro from the project directory and run:
```
/chat load ~/john-project-session.json
```
Or use `kiro-cli chat --resume` to pick up the most recent session automatically (sessions are saved per-directory, so as long as you start Kiro from `/Users/billsneddon/Data/personal/Church/SS/John` it will remember).

The `--resume` flag is probably the easiest — just run `kiro-cli chat --resume` from the project directory and it'll restore the last conversation automatically.

```
/chat save /Users/billsneddon/Data/personal/Church/SS/John/kiro-session.json
```

content.data:
As suspected — the files are revealjs (slide) format, not document format. That's why PDF rendering didn't work; LaTeX can't render revealjs slides.

You have two options:

1. **Print-to-PDF from browser** — open the HTML slide in a browser, add `?print-pdf` to the URL, then print to PDF. This is the standard revealjs approach and preserves the slide layout.

2. **Create a separate document-format `.qmd`** for each lesson that renders to PDF — essentially a plain document version of the same content.

Given your content uses collapsible `<details>` HTML elements, option 1 (browser print-to-PDF) is probably the most practical. Would you like to go that route, or would you prefer a separate document format for sharing?
so if I do a browser print to pdf would I have to open all of the colapesed content?

Yes — `<details>` elements only print what's currently open. Collapsed sections would appear as just the summary line in the PDF.

The cleanest solution for sharing with students would be a separate document-format `.qmd` that renders to HTML (or PDF) with all content visible and no collapsing. You'd essentially have:

- `introduction.qmd` → revealjs slides (for teaching)
- `introduction-handout.qmd` → plain document (for students)

The handout could reuse the same content but formatted as a simple readable document. Want me to create one for the Introduction lesson as a template you could follow for the others?
can I just have you create the handout qmd from the lesson qmd and then generate the pdf. Will it require a seperate quarto render command for each lesson?

I can create handout `.qmd` files from each lesson and add them to `_quarto.yml` so a single `quarto render` generates everything at once.

Let me look at the full introduction.qmd first to understand the content structure before building the converter.

input.__tool_use_purpose: Read the full introduction.qmd

content.data:
--- format: revealjs: theme: default css: ../course-outline.css slide-number: true center: false margin: 0.05 scrollable: true footer: "← Back" --- ```{=html}

I. Introduction

A. Overview — Watch Video
1. Bible Project — The Book of John
a. Song Jesus Name above all names
b. bibleproject.com/guides/book-of-john 1-12/
c. bibleproject.com/guides/book-of-john 13-21/
B. The Book
1. Authorship and Date
a. Church Fathers' Views (2nd–4th centuries AD) — unanimous attribution to John the Apostle
— Written in Ephesus late in John's life, after the Synoptic Gospels had circulated
— Often called a "spiritual Gospel" emphasizing deeper theological truths
— Irenaeus (c. 180 AD, disciple of Polycarp who knew the apostles): "John, the disciple of the Lord… did himself publish a Gospel during his residence at Ephesus in Asia"
— Clement of Alexandria (c. 215 AD): urged by friends and inspired by the Spirit to write a "spiritual Gospel" after his oral ministry in Asia
— Origen (c. 185–254 AD) and Eusebius (c. 260–340 AD): both affirmed apostolic authorship; listed as undisputed in the church canon
— Also attested by Tertullian, the Muratorian Canon, and Justin Martyr
— Small dissenting group (the Alogi) rejected it for theological reasons — not the mainstream view
b. Modern Scholarly Views on Authorship
— The Gospel is formally anonymous — never names its author
— Refers to the "disciple whom Jesus loved" as source of testimony (John 21:20–24; 13:23)
— Majority consensus: not directly authored by John the Apostle; product of a Johannine community or "school"
— Reasons: sophisticated Greek style, strong Judean focus, distinct theology vs. Synoptics
— The Beloved Disciple was a real historical figure; exact identity unknown (candidates: John, Lazarus, a Jerusalem priest)
— Minority/traditionalist view: Leon Morris, John A.T. Robinson, Richard Bauckham support direct or close connection to John the Apostle
c. Modern Scholarly Views on Date
— Broad consensus: c. 90–100 AD — latest of the four canonical Gospels, likely composed in Ephesus
— Earliest manuscript (P52) dated c. 125–150 AD sets the upper limit
— Gnostic writers like Heracleon commented on it by the mid-2nd century
— Minority: some conservatives (Robinson, Wright, Bauckham) argue pre-70 AD based on detailed Jerusalem topography and no explicit reference to Temple's fall
—Very late 2nd-century dates largely faded after manuscript evidence (e.g., P52 ~125–150 CE) showed the text already existed early in the 2nd century. Today’s critical consensus (including Ehrman) favors late 1st century (~90–100 CE), not mid-2nd.
d. Passage missing from early manuscripts
— John 7:53–8:11 (the story of the woman caught in adultery, or Pericope Adulterae) Not present in any Greek manuscripts before the 5th century; style and vocabulary differ from the rest of John. Modern translations usually bracket it or footnote: “The earliest manuscripts do not include 7:53–8:11.”
2. Stated Purpose
a. John 20:31 — "…but these are written so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in his name."
3. Distinctives of John
a. ~80–90% of content is unique — not found in Matthew, Mark, or Luke
b. Seven "I AM" Statements
1) John 6:35 — "I am the bread of life"
2) John 8:12 — "I am the light of the world"
3) John 10:7,9 — "I am the door/gate"
4) John 10:11,14 — "I am the good shepherd"
5) John 11:25 — "I am the resurrection and the life"
6) John 14:6 — "I am the way, the truth, and the life"
7) John 15:1,5 — "I am the true vine"
c. Seven Signs
1) John 2:1–11 — Water into wine (Cana)
2) John 4:43–54 — Healing of the official's son
3) John 5:1–15 — Healing at the pool of Bethesda
4) John 6:1–14 — Feeding of the five thousand
5) John 6:16–21 — Jesus walking on water
6) John 9:1–41 — Healing of the man born blind
7) John 11:1–44 — Raising of Lazarus
d. Seven Titles of Jesus in John 1
1) Lamb of God — John 1:29 (declared by John the Baptist)
2) Rabbi / Teacher — John 1:38 (used by the first disciples)
3) Messiah / Christ — John 1:41 (proclaimed by Andrew)
4) Jesus of Nazareth — John 1:45 (identified by Philip)
5) Son of God — John 1:49 (confessed by Nathanael)
6) King of Israel — John 1:49 (also confessed by Nathanael)
7) Son of Man — John 1:51 (Jesus' own self-designation)
e. Teaching on Holy spirit John 14,16
1) Holy spirit teaching — John 14-16 (covered in future lesson)
f. High Preistly Prayer John 17
1) Preistly Prayer — John 17 (covered in future lesson)
C. About the Author
1. Life of John
a. Born c. AD 6–15, Galilee (likely Bethsaida or Capernaum); son of Zebedee and Salome; brother of James the apostle
b. Called among the first disciples; inner circle with Peter and James
c. The "Beloved Disciple" — remained at the crucifixion; entrusted with Mary's care
d. Pillar of the Jerusalem church (Galatians 2:9); founded churches in Asia Minor
e. Exiled to Patmos under Emperor Domitian (c. AD 95) — wrote Revelation there
f. Released under Nerva (AD 96–98); returned to Ephesus
g. Died naturally in Ephesus c. AD 100 — only apostle not martyred
2. Christian Tradition
a. Attributed authorship: Gospel of John, 1–3 John, Revelation
b. Known for teaching: "Little children, love one another" (Jerome)
c. Plunged into boiling oil in Rome under Domitian — emerged unharmed; then exiled to Patmos
— Earliest written reference: Tertullian (c. 200 AD), who mentions it as a well-known miracle
d. Fled Ephesian bathhouse on seeing the heretic Cerinthus
e. Disciple chain: John → Polycarp → Irenaeus — key line of apostolic tradition
3. John in the Gospels and Acts
a. Early Ministry
— Called by the Sea of Galilee with brother James (Matt 4:21–22; Mark 1:19–20; Luke 5:10)
— Named "Sons of Thunder" (Boanerges) by Jesus (Mark 3:17)
— Inner circle witness to raising of Jairus' daughter (Mark 5:37; Luke 8:51)
— Sent out with the Twelve (Matt 10; Mark 6:7; Luke 9:1)
— Asked to call down fire on a Samaritan village; Jesus rebukes him (Luke 9:51–56)
b. Mid-Ministry
— Witnesses the Transfiguration with Peter and James (Matt 17:1–9; Mark 9:2–10; Luke 9:28–36)
— Reports stopping an unauthorized exorcist; Jesus corrects him (Luke 9:49–50)
— Requests seats of honor in Christ's kingdom (Matt 20:20–28; Mark 10:35–45)
c. Passion Week
— Sent with Peter to prepare the Passover meal (Luke 22:8)
— The "Beloved Disciple" reclining next to Jesus at the Last Supper (John 13:23–25)
— Among the three in Gethsemane during Jesus' agony (Matt 26:36–37; Mark 14:32–33)
— Gains access to the high priest's courtyard during Jesus' trial (John 18:15–16)
— Present at the cross; Jesus entrusts Mary to his care (John 19:25–27)
d. Resurrection Events
— Runs with Peter to the empty tomb and believes (John 20:2–8)
— First to recognize the risen Jesus by the Sea of Galilee (John 21:7)
e. In the Book of Acts
— Listed among apostles waiting in the Upper Room (Acts 1:13)
— Accompanies Peter in healing the lame man at the Temple (Acts 3:1–11)
— Stands boldly with Peter before the Sanhedrin (Acts 4:1–22)
— Lays hands on new Samaritan believers with Peter (Acts 8:14–17)
f. Summary
— Consistently part of the inner circle (Peter, James, John)
— Present at key revelation moments: Transfiguration and Resurrection
— Witness to the full Passion narrative
— Early leader in the Jerusalem church
```

The content is entirely raw HTML inside a `{=html}` block. Writing a parser for this would be complex and error-prone. A much better approach: I'll write a Python script that strips the HTML tags and converts the structure to clean Markdown, then generates a handout `.qmd` with `format: html` (or `pdf`).

content.data: Good — consistent structure. I'll write a Python script to parse the HTML and generate handout `.qmd` files for all lessons, then add them to `_quarto.yml`.

input.__tool_use_purpose: Create Python script to generate handout qmd files from lesson qmd files
input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env python3 """Convert revealjs lesson .qmd files to PDF-friendly handout .qmd files.""" import re from pathlib import Path from html.parser import HTMLParser LESSONS = [ "01-Intro/introduction.qmd", "02 - Jesus as God/jesus-as-god.qmd", "03 - Jesus in Creation/jesus-in-creation.qmd", "04 - Jesus aa life and light/jesus-life-light.qmd", "05 - Jesus in Salvation/jesus-in-salvation.qmd", "05 - Jesus grace truth and the law/grace-truth-law.qmd", "06 - Jesus and the Holy Spirit/holy-spirit.qmd", "07 - Jesus the Only way to the Father/only-way.qmd", "08 - Jesus priestly prayer/priestly-prayer.qmd", ] INDENT_MAP = { "h2": ("# ", ""), "strong": ("**", "**"), # inside summary → heading } # CSS class → indent prefix CLASS_INDENT = { "lv-1": " ", "lv-1-gap": " ", "lv-lc": " ", "lv-lc-italic": " ", "lv-detail": " ", } class HandoutParser(HTMLParser): def __init__(self): super().__init__() self.lines = [] self._stack = [] # tag stack self._summary_depth = 0 self._in_summary = False self._summary_level = 0 # nesting depth of
self._details_depth = 0 self._current_class = None self._buf = "" def handle_starttag(self, tag, attrs): attrs = dict(attrs) self._stack.append(tag) if tag == "details": self._details_depth += 1 elif tag == "summary": self._in_summary = True self._summary_level = self._details_depth self._buf = "" elif tag in ("div",): self._current_class = attrs.get("class", "") self._buf = "" elif tag == "h2": self._buf = "" elif tag == "a": self._buf += "" # links: just keep text def handle_endtag(self, tag): if self._stack and self._stack[-1] == tag: self._stack.pop() if tag == "summary": self._in_summary = False text = self._buf.strip() level = self._summary_level if level == 1: self.lines.append(f"\n## {text}\n") elif level == 2: self.lines.append(f"\n### {text}\n") else: self.lines.append(f"\n#### {text}\n") self._buf = "" elif tag == "div": text = self._buf.strip() if text: indent = CLASS_INDENT.get(self._current_class, " ") self.lines.append(f"{indent}{text}\n") self._buf = "" self._current_class = None elif tag == "h2": text = self._buf.strip() if text: self.lines.append(f"# {text}\n\n") self._buf = "" elif tag == "details": self._details_depth -= 1 def handle_data(self, data): if self._in_summary or self._current_class or (self._stack and self._stack[-1] == "h2"): self._buf += data def get_markdown(self): return "".join(self.lines) def extract_title(qmd_text): m = re.search(r"]*>(.*?)", qmd_text, re.DOTALL) if m: return re.sub(r"<[^>]+>", "", m.group(1)).strip() return "Lesson" def convert(src_path: Path) -> str: text = src_path.read_text() # Extract the HTML block m = re.search(r"```\{=html\}(.*?)```", text, re.DOTALL) if not m: return "" html = m.group(1) title = extract_title(html) parser = HandoutParser() parser.feed(html) md = parser.get_markdown() header = f"""--- title: "{title}" format: pdf: toc: false number-sections: false geometry: margin=1in fontsize: 11pt --- """ return header + md def main(): base = Path(__file__).parent handout_paths = [] for lesson in LESSONS: src = base / lesson if not src.exists(): print(f"SKIP (not found): {lesson}") continue stem = src.stem out = src.parent / f"{stem}-handout.qmd" content = convert(src) if not content: print(f"SKIP (no html block): {lesson}") continue out.write_text(content) print(f"Created: {out.relative_to(base)}") handout_paths.append(str(out.relative_to(base))) # Update _quarto.yml render list yml_path = base / "_quarto.yml" yml = yml_path.read_text() # Remove old handout entries if any yml = re.sub(r'\s*- "?[^"\n]*-handout\.qmd"?\n', "", yml) # Remove pdf: default if present (handouts have their own format) yml = re.sub(r'\s*pdf: default\n', "\n", yml) # Append handout paths to render list additions = "" for p in handout_paths: q = f'"{p}"' if " " in p else p additions += f" - {q}\n" yml = yml.rstrip() + "\n" + additions yml_path.write_text(yml) print(f"\nUpdated _quarto.yml with {len(handout_paths)} handout(s).") if __name__ == "__main__": main()

tool.tool_use_purpose: Create Python script to generate handout qmd files from lesson qmd files

FileWrite.content:
#!/usr/bin/env python3 """Convert revealjs lesson .qmd files to PDF-friendly handout .qmd files.""" import re from pathlib import Path from html.parser import HTMLParser LESSONS = [ "01-Intro/introduction.qmd", "02 - Jesus as God/jesus-as-god.qmd", "03 - Jesus in Creation/jesus-in-creation.qmd", "04 - Jesus aa life and light/jesus-life-light.qmd", "05 - Jesus in Salvation/jesus-in-salvation.qmd", "05 - Jesus grace truth and the law/grace-truth-law.qmd", "06 - Jesus and the Holy Spirit/holy-spirit.qmd", "07 - Jesus the Only way to the Father/only-way.qmd", "08 - Jesus priestly prayer/priestly-prayer.qmd", ] INDENT_MAP = { "h2": ("# ", ""), "strong": ("**", "**"), # inside summary → heading } # CSS class → indent prefix CLASS_INDENT = { "lv-1": " ", "lv-1-gap": " ", "lv-lc": " ", "lv-lc-italic": " ", "lv-detail": " ", } class HandoutParser(HTMLParser): def __init__(self): super().__init__() self.lines = [] self._stack = [] # tag stack self._summary_depth = 0 self._in_summary = False self._summary_level = 0 # nesting depth of
self._details_depth = 0 self._current_class = None self._buf = "" def handle_starttag(self, tag, attrs): attrs = dict(attrs) self._stack.append(tag) if tag == "details": self._details_depth += 1 elif tag == "summary": self._in_summary = True self._summary_level = self._details_depth self._buf = "" elif tag in ("div",): self._current_class = attrs.get("class", "") self._buf = "" elif tag == "h2": self._buf = "" elif tag == "a": self._buf += "" # links: just keep text def handle_endtag(self, tag): if self._stack and self._stack[-1] == tag: self._stack.pop() if tag == "summary": self._in_summary = False text = self._buf.strip() level = self._summary_level if level == 1: self.lines.append(f"\n## {text}\n") elif level == 2: self.lines.append(f"\n### {text}\n") else: self.lines.append(f"\n#### {text}\n") self._buf = "" elif tag == "div": text = self._buf.strip() if text: indent = CLASS_INDENT.get(self._current_class, " ") self.lines.append(f"{indent}{text}\n") self._buf = "" self._current_class = None elif tag == "h2": text = self._buf.strip() if text: self.lines.append(f"# {text}\n\n") self._buf = "" elif tag == "details": self._details_depth -= 1 def handle_data(self, data): if self._in_summary or self._current_class or (self._stack and self._stack[-1] == "h2"): self._buf += data def get_markdown(self): return "".join(self.lines) def extract_title(qmd_text): m = re.search(r"]*>(.*?)", qmd_text, re.DOTALL) if m: return re.sub(r"<[^>]+>", "", m.group(1)).strip() return "Lesson" def convert(src_path: Path) -> str: text = src_path.read_text() # Extract the HTML block m = re.search(r"```\{=html\}(.*?)```", text, re.DOTALL) if not m: return "" html = m.group(1) title = extract_title(html) parser = HandoutParser() parser.feed(html) md = parser.get_markdown() header = f"""--- title: "{title}" format: pdf: toc: false number-sections: false geometry: margin=1in fontsize: 11pt --- """ return header + md def main(): base = Path(__file__).parent handout_paths = [] for lesson in LESSONS: src = base / lesson if not src.exists(): print(f"SKIP (not found): {lesson}") continue stem = src.stem out = src.parent / f"{stem}-handout.qmd" content = convert(src) if not content: print(f"SKIP (no html block): {lesson}") continue out.write_text(content) print(f"Created: {out.relative_to(base)}") handout_paths.append(str(out.relative_to(base))) # Update _quarto.yml render list yml_path = base / "_quarto.yml" yml = yml_path.read_text() # Remove old handout entries if any yml = re.sub(r'\s*- "?[^"\n]*-handout\.qmd"?\n', "", yml) # Remove pdf: default if present (handouts have their own format) yml = re.sub(r'\s*pdf: default\n', "\n", yml) # Append handout paths to render list additions = "" for p in handout_paths: q = f'"{p}"' if " " in p else p additions += f" - {q}\n" yml = yml.rstrip() + "\n" + additions yml_path.write_text(yml) print(f"\nUpdated _quarto.yml with {len(handout_paths)} handout(s).") if __name__ == "__main__": main()

content.data:
### 3. Distinctives of John
  a. ~80–90% of content is unique — not found in Matthew, Mark, or Luke
  b. Seven "I AM" Statements
    1) John 6:35 — "I am the bread of life"
    2) John 8:12 — "I am the light of the world"
    3) John 10:7,9 — "I am the door/gate"
    4) John 10:11,14 — "I am the good shepherd"
    5) John 11:25 — "I am the resurrection and the life"
    6) John 14:6 — "I am the way, the truth, and the life"
    7) John 15:1,5 — "I am the true vine"
  c. Seven Signs
    1) John 2:1–11 — Water into wine (Cana)
    2) John 4:43–54 — Healing of the official's son
    3) John 5:1–15 — Healing at the pool of Bethesda
    4) John 6:1–14 — Feeding of the five thousand
    5) John 6:16–21 — Jesus walking on water
    6) John 9:1–41 — Healing of the man born blind
    7) John 11:1–44 — Raising of Lazarus
  d. Seven Titles of Jesus in John 1
    1) Lamb of God — John 1:29 (declared by John the Baptist)
    2) Rabbi / Teacher — John 1:38 (used by the first disciples)
    3) Messiah / Christ — John 1:41 (proclaimed by Andrew)
    4) Jesus of Nazareth — John 1:45 (identified by Philip)
    5) Son of God — John 1:49 (confessed by Nathanael)
    6) King of Israel — John 1:49 (also confessed by Nathanael)
    7) Son of Man — John 1:51 (Jesus' own self-designation)
  e. Teaching on Holy spirit John 14,16
    1) Holy spirit teaching — John 14-16 (covered in future lesson)
  f. High Preistly Prayer John 17
    1) Preistly Prayer — John 17 (covered in future lesson)

content.data:
But plain indented lines will still trigger code blocks at 4 spaces. I'll switch to using a LaTeX-friendly approach — write the content as a `\hangindent` block via raw LaTeX, or simpler: just use `\quad` spacing in the text itself. Actually the cleanest fix is to output each line followed by `\` (a Pandoc line break) so they stay as flowing paragraphs with a visual indent via non-breaking spaces, but without the 4-space code block trigger.

Let me just use `\hspace` via a simpler approach — write lines as plain paragraphs with a unicode em-space prefix for indentation:

input.__tool_use_purpose: Use unicode indent characters to avoid code block triggering
input.newStr:
# CSS class → indent prefix (using unicode thin spaces to avoid pandoc code-block trigger)
CLASS_INDENT = {
    "lv-1":         "",
    "lv-1-gap":     "",
    "lv-lc":        "\u2003",        # 1 em-space
    "lv-lc-italic": "\u2003",
    "lv-detail":    "\u2003\u2003",  # 2 em-spaces
}
input.oldStr:

All committed and pushed. Everything is now saved to GitHub:
- `make_handouts.py` — the handout generator script
- All 9 `*-handout.qmd` files
- Updated `_quarto.yml` and `.gitignore`
- Fixed `introduction.qmd`

To regenerate handouts in a future session, just run `python3 make_handouts.py` from the project directory.
okay what is the procedure going forward to I need to save some context so you remember how to create notes pdf and qmd for future lessions?

I don't retain memory between sessions — each new chat starts fresh. But the good news is everything you need is already in the repo, so the procedure is simple:

**When you add a new lesson:**
1. Create your lesson `.qmd` (e.g. `09 - New Lesson/new-lesson.qmd`)
2. Add it to the `render:` list in `_quarto.yml`
3. Add it to the `LESSONS` list in `make_handouts.py`
4. Run `python3 make_handouts.py` — generates the handout `.qmd`
5. Run `quarto render` — builds all slides + handout PDFs

**To remind me in a future session**, just say something like:
> "I'm working in `/Users/billsneddon/Data/personal/Church/SS/John` — check `make_handouts.py` and `_quarto.yml` to understand the project setup."

That's all I need — the code is self-documenting enough that I can pick up from there.

data.documentation:
--- Documentation for: save load chat session commands ---
The following documentation is provided inline. DO NOT attempt to read files - all content is below.



--- Document 1 ---
--- doc_meta: validated: 2026-04-09 commit: 4ae084db status: validated testable_headless: false category: slash_command title: /chat load description: Load previously saved conversation from file to resume session keywords: [chat, load, import, restore, resume, session, zip, json] related: [chat-save, chat-new] --- # /chat load Load previously saved conversation from file to resume session. ## Overview The `/chat load` command imports a conversation from a saved session file. Supports multiple file formats including the native Kiro export format, zip archives, and legacy V1 sessions. ## Usage ``` /chat load <path> ``` ## Parameters | Parameter | Required | Description | |-----------|----------|-------------| | `path` | Yes | Path to saved session file (.json or .zip) | ## Supported Formats | Format | Description | |--------|-------------| | Kiro | `kiro-session-export-v1` JSON (created by `/chat save`) | | Zip | Archive containing `session_metadata.json` + `conversation_log.jsonl` | | Legacy | V1 `ConversationState` JSON from older CLI versions | | SessionDataOnly | Bare `SessionData` JSON with optional companion `.jsonl` file | ## How It Works 1. Reads the file and auto-detects format 2. Extracts session metadata and conversation log 3. Creates a new session with a fresh UUID 4. Sets `imported_from` field to track origin The loaded session uses your current: - Agent configuration - Model configuration - MCP connections - Hooks ## Examples ### Example 1: Load from JSON File ``` /chat load my-session.json ``` **Output**: ``` Loaded session from /home/user/my-session.json ``` ### Example 2: Load from Zip Archive ``` /chat load backup.zip ``` ### Example 3: Load with Extension Fallback ``` /chat load backup ``` Tries `backup` first, then `backup.zip`, then `backup.json` if not found. ### Example 4: Load from Absolute Path ``` /chat load ~/backups/important-conversation.json ``` ## Troubleshooting ### Issue: File Not Found **Symptom**: "Failed to read" error **Cause**: File doesn't exist at path **Solution**: Check path. Command tries `.zip` then `.json` extensions automatically. ### Issue: Unrecognized Format **Symptom**: "Unrecognized session file format" error **Cause**: File is not a valid session export **Solution**: Ensure file was created with `/chat save` or is a valid legacy export. ### Issue: Invalid Zip Archive **Symptom**: "File has .zip extension but is not a valid zip archive" **Cause**: Corrupted or invalid zip file **Solution**: Re-export the session or use JSON format. ## Related Features - [/chat save](chat-save.md) - Save conversation to file - [/chat new](chat-new.md) - Start a new conversation - [kiro-cli chat --resume](../commands/chat.md) - Resume last conversation ## Technical Details **Extension Handling**: If path has no extension and file not found, tries `.zip` first, then `.json`. **Session ID**: Loaded sessions get a new UUID. The original path is stored in `imported_from`. **Format Detection**: Uses file extension for `.zip`, then probes JSON structure for other formats.

--- Document 2 ---
--- doc_meta: validated: 2026-04-09 commit: 4ae084db status: validated testable_headless: false category: slash_command title: /chat save description: Save current conversation to file for later resumption keywords: [chat, save, export, persist, session] related: [chat-load, chat-new] --- # /chat save Save current conversation to file for later resumption. ## Overview The `/chat save` command exports the current conversation state to a JSON file. Conversations are automatically saved to the database per-directory, but this command allows explicit file exports for backup, sharing, or custom storage. ## Usage ``` /chat save <path> /chat save <path> --force ``` ## Parameters | Parameter | Required | Description | |-----------|----------|-------------| | `path` | Yes | File path where conversation will be saved | | `--force`, `-f` | No | Overwrite existing file without prompting | ## How It Works Exports current conversation state as `kiro-session-export-v1` JSON including: - Session metadata (session ID, working directory, timestamps, title) - Complete log entries (message history) File can be loaded later with `/chat load` to restore the conversation. ## Examples ### Example 1: Save to File ``` /chat save my-session.json ``` **Output**: ``` Saved session to my-session.json ``` ### Example 2: Save Without Extension If no extension is provided, `.json` is automatically added: ``` /chat save my-session ``` Saves to `my-session.json`. ### Example 3: Overwrite Existing File ``` /chat save backup.json --force ``` ### Example 4: Save with Path ``` /chat save ~/backups/important-conversation.json ``` ## Troubleshooting ### Issue: File Already Exists **Symptom**: Error "File already exists" **Cause**: File at path already exists and --force not used **Solution**: Use `--force` flag or choose different path ### Issue: Permission Denied **Symptom**: "Failed to write" error **Cause**: No write permission for path **Solution**: Check directory permissions or use different path ### Issue: Invalid Path **Symptom**: Export fails **Cause**: Parent directory doesn't exist **Solution**: Create parent directories first or use existing path ## Related Features - [/chat load](chat-load.md) - Load saved conversation - [/chat new](chat-new.md) - Start fresh conversation - [kiro-cli chat](../commands/chat.md) - CLI session management ## Technical Details **File Format**: `kiro-session-export-v1` JSON envelope **Structure**: ```json { "format": "kiro-session-export-v1", "metadata": { "session_id": "...", "cwd": "/path/to/dir", "created_at": "2026-01-01T00:00:00Z", "updated_at": "2026-01-01T00:00:00Z", "title": "Session Title", "session_state": "..." }, "log_entries": [...] } ``` **State Preserved**: - Session ID - Working directory (cwd) - Created/updated timestamps - Session title - Log entries (complete message history) **State Not Preserved**: - Tool manager instance (recreated on load) - MCP connections (reconnected on load) - Agent configuration (uses current session's config) **Auto-Extension**: `.json` extension added automatically if no extension provided.

--- Document 3 ---
--- doc_meta: validated: 2026-04-09 commit: 4ae084db status: validated testable_headless: false category: feature title: Session Management description: Automatic session saving, resumption, and file-based storage keywords: [session, save, load, resume, auto-save, storage] related: [chat-save, chat-load, chat] --- # Session Management Automatic session saving, resumption, and file-based storage. ## Overview Kiro CLI automatically saves all chat sessions on every conversation turn. Sessions are stored per-directory as files. Resume from any previous session or export to portable files. ## Auto-Save **Automatic**: Every conversation turn saved **Scope**: Per-directory (each project has own sessions) **Storage**: `~/.kiro/sessions/cli/` - `{session_id}.json` - metadata (cwd, timestamps, session state) - `{session_id}.jsonl` - append-only conversation log - `{session_id}.lock` - lock file (exists only when session is active) **Session ID**: UUID for each session ## Managing Sessions ### From Command Line ```bash # Resume most recent session kiro-cli chat --resume # Interactive picker kiro-cli chat --resume-picker # List all sessions kiro-cli chat --list-sessions # Delete session kiro-cli chat --delete-session <SESSION_ID> ``` ### From Chat ```bash # Save to file /chat save <path> # Load from file /chat load <path> ``` **Note**: `.json` extension optional when loading. ## Session Storage **Files**: Sessions auto-saved per-directory to `~/.kiro/sessions/cli/` **Export**: Manual export via `/chat save` **Session ID**: UUID format (e.g., `f2946a26-3735-4b08-8d05-c928010302d5`) ## Examples ### Example 1: Resume Last Session ```bash kiro-cli chat --resume ``` Continues most recent conversation, restoring the model that was active when the session was saved. ### Example 2: Pick Session ```bash kiro-cli chat --resume-picker ``` Shows list of sessions to choose from. ### Example 3: Export to File ``` /chat save backup.json ``` Exports current session to file. ### Example 4: Save and Load Workflow ```bash # Save current session /chat save ./backup.json # Later, load it back /chat load ./backup.json ``` ## Troubleshooting ### Issue: No Sessions to Resume **Symptom**: "No saved chat sessions" **Cause**: No sessions in current directory **Solution**: Sessions are per-directory. Navigate to correct directory. ## Related - [/chat save](../slash-commands/chat-save.md) - Save command - [/chat load](../slash-commands/chat-load.md) - Load command - [kiro-cli chat](../commands/chat.md) - CLI options ## Limitations - Sessions stored per-directory - Auto-save to files only (not cloud) - Session IDs are UUIDs (not human-readable) - No cloud sync built-in - No session search by content ## Technical Details **Storage**: File-based in `~/.kiro/sessions/cli/` **Scope**: Sessions keyed by directory path **Auto-Save**: After every conversation turn **Model Preservation**: When resuming via `--resume`, the model active when the session was saved is restored. This includes models switched mid-session with `/model`. The `--model` CLI flag overrides the saved model.

--- Document 4 ---
--- doc_meta: validated: 2026-03-22 commit: b8a43f49 status: validated testable_headless: false category: slash_command title: /chat new description: Start a fresh conversation without restarting the CLI keywords: [chat, new, fresh, conversation, reset, start over] related: [chat-load, chat-save, clear] --- # /chat new Start a fresh conversation without restarting the CLI. ## Overview The `/chat new` command creates a brand new conversation session, clearing all messages and starting fresh. The previous session is preserved and can be resumed later with `/chat`. Creates a new ACP session and terminates the old one. ## Usage ### Start a fresh conversation ``` /chat new ``` ### Start with an initial prompt ``` /chat new <prompt> ``` Immediately sends the prompt to the new conversation after creation. ## Examples ### Basic usage ``` > /chat new ✔ New conversation started. Use /chat to return to previous sessions. ``` ### With an initial prompt ``` > /chat new explain how async works in Rust ``` This creates a new conversation and immediately sends "explain how async works in Rust" as the first message. ## How It Works 1. The current session is saved automatically 2. A new session is created with a fresh conversation ID 3. All messages are cleared from the display 4. Model and agent settings are preserved 5. MCP servers remain available (creates new server instances) ## Differences from /clear | Feature | `/chat new` | `/clear` | |---------|-------------|----------| | Creates new session ID | Yes | No | | Previous session resumable via `/chat` | Yes | No | | Clears conversation history | Yes | Yes | | Resets context window | Yes | Yes | | Preserves model selection | Yes | Yes | | Preserves agent selection | Yes | Yes | ## Related - `/chat` — List and load previous sessions - `/chat save` — Save conversation to file - `/chat load` — Load conversation from file - `/clear` — Clear conversation without creating new session ## Limitations - MCP servers are re-initialized in the new session (brief startup delay)

--- Document 5 ---
--- doc_meta: validated: 2026-03-24 commit: 21e95839 status: validated testable_headless: true category: command title: kiro-cli chat description: Start AI assistant session with support for agents, models, tool trust, and conversation management keywords: [chat, conversation, agent, model, interactive, headless, mcp, log, logging, history, KIRO_LOG_NO_COLOR] related: [slash-chat-save, slash-chat-load, slash-agent, exit-codes] --- # kiro-cli chat Start AI assistant session with support for agents, models, tool trust, and conversation management. ## Overview The chat command launches an interactive AI assistant session. Supports agent selection, model choice, tool trust configuration, conversation resumption, and headless mode for automation. Primary interface for interacting with Kiro CLI. ## Usage ### Basic Usage ```bash kiro-cli chat ``` ### Common Use Cases #### Use Case 1: Start Interactive Session ```bash kiro-cli chat ``` **What this does**: Launches interactive chat session with default agent and model. #### Use Case 2: Start with Specific Agent ```bash kiro-cli chat --agent rust-expert ``` **What this does**: Starts session using rust-expert agent configuration. #### Use Case 3: Headless Mode with Query ```bash kiro-cli chat --no-interactive "List all Rust files in src/" ``` **What this does**: Executes single query non-interactively and exits. #### Use Case 4: Trust All Tools ```bash kiro-cli chat --trust-all-tools "Run tests and analyze results" ``` **What this does**: Allows agent to use any tool without approval prompts. #### Use Case 5: Trust Specific Tools ```bash kiro-cli chat --trust-tools=fs_read,grep "Find all TODOs" ``` **What this does**: Auto-approves only fs_read and grep tools. #### Use Case 6: Resume Last Conversation ```bash kiro-cli chat --resume ``` **What this does**: Resumes most recent conversation from current directory. Restores the model that was active when the session was saved (e.g., if you switched models with `/model`). #### Use Case 7: Select Conversation to Resume ```bash kiro-cli chat --resume-picker ``` **What this does**: Shows interactive picker to select conversation to resume. ## Options | Option | Short | Type | Description | |--------|-------|------|-------------| | `--resume` | `-r` | flag | Resume most recent conversation (restores saved model) | | `--resume-id` | | string | Resume a specific conversation by session ID | | `--resume-picker` | | flag | Interactively select conversation to resume | | `--agent` | | string | Agent to use (default: default agent) | | `--model` | | string | Model to use (default: default model) | | `--trust-all-tools` | `-a` | flag | Auto-approve all tool uses | | `--trust-tools` | | list | Auto-approve specific tools (comma-separated) | | `--no-interactive` | | flag | Run without user input (headless mode) | | `--list-sessions` | `-l` | flag | List saved conversations | | `--delete-session` | `-d` | string | Delete conversation by ID | | `--wrap` | `-w` | enum | Line wrapping (always/never/auto) | | `--legacy-mode` | | flag | Use legacy terminal UI instead of embedded TUI | | `--verbose` | `-v` | flag | Increase logging verbosity (can be repeated) | | `--help` | `-h` | flag | Print help information | | `[INPUT]` | | string | Initial query to send | ## Examples ### Example 1: Quick Query ```bash kiro-cli chat "What files are in the current directory?" ``` ### Example 2: Automated Script ```bash kiro-cli chat --no-interactive --trust-all-tools "Run cargo test and summarize results" ``` ### Example 3: Specific Model ```bash kiro-cli chat --model <model-id> "Explain this codebase" ``` ### Example 4: List Conversations ```bash kiro-cli chat --list-sessions ``` **Expected Output**: ``` Chat sessions for /path/to/project: Chat SessionId: f2946a26-3735-4b08-8d05-c928010302d5 2 hours ago | Implement user authentication | 15 msgs Chat SessionId: 7bd2c90f-7080-4981-87f7-206d9147878f 1 days ago | Refactor database layer | 23 msgs To delete a session, use: kiro-cli chat --delete-session <SESSION_ID> ``` ### Example 5: Delete Conversation ```bash kiro-cli chat --delete-session abc123 ``` **Expected Output**: ``` ✔ Deleted chat session abc123 ``` ## Headless Mode Use `--no-interactive` for automation and scripts: **Requirements**: - Must provide initial query as argument - Use `--trust-all-tools` or `--trust-tools` to avoid hanging on tool approvals - Interactive slash commands won't work (/model picker, /agent picker) - No mid-session user input possible **What Works**: - Natural language queries - Tool invocations (with trust flags) - Informational slash commands (/help, /tools) - Settings via `kiro-cli settings` command **What Doesn't Work**: - Interactive slash commands requiring selection - Tool approval prompts (will hang) - Mid-session confirmations - Terminal UI features ## Troubleshooting ### Issue: Tool Approval Hangs in Headless Mode **Symptom**: Command hangs waiting for input **Cause**: Tool requires approval but no interactive input available **Solution**: Use `--trust-all-tools` or `--trust-tools=tool1,tool2` ### Issue: Can't Resume Conversation **Symptom**: --resume shows no conversations **Cause**: No saved conversations in current directory **Solution**: Conversations are directory-specific. Use `/chat save` to save conversations. ### Issue: Agent Not Found **Symptom**: Error about agent not existing **Cause**: Specified agent doesn't exist **Solution**: Check agent name. Use `kiro-cli agent list` to see available agents. ### Issue: Interactive Command Fails in Headless **Symptom**: Error "not a terminal" **Cause**: Interactive slash command used in headless mode **Solution**: Use direct CLI commands instead (e.g., `kiro-cli settings`) ### Issue: MCP Server Startup Failure **Symptom**: MCP servers fail to start **Cause**: One or more MCP servers failed to start **Solution**: Check MCP server configuration. Verify server paths and dependencies are correct. ## Related Features - [/chat save](../slash-commands/chat-save.md) - Save conversations - [/chat load](../slash-commands/chat-load.md) - Load conversations - [/agent](../slash-commands/agent-swap.md) - Switch agents mid-session - [kiro-cli agent](agent.md) - Manage agent configurations - [kiro-cli settings](settings.md) - Configure behavior ## Limitations - Conversations saved per-directory - Headless mode doesn't support interactive commands - Tool trust applies to entire session - Can't change trust settings mid-session - Resume only works for conversations in current directory ## Technical Details **Aliases**: `chat` (default command if no subcommand specified) **Conversation Storage**: Saved in database, scoped to current directory path. **Agent Resolution**: Local agents (`.kiro/agents/`) take precedence over global (`~/.kiro/agents/`). **Model Selection**: Uses specified model or default from settings. Can be changed mid-session with `/model`. When resuming a session with `--resume`, the model active when the session was saved is restored (unless overridden with `--model`). **Tool Trust**: - `--trust-all-tools`: Bypasses all tool approval prompts - `--trust-tools=`: Empty list trusts no tools - `--trust-tools=a,b`: Trusts only tools a and b - Without flags: Prompts for each tool use (unless in agent's allowedTools) **Headless Mode**: Sets non-interactive flag, disables terminal UI, requires initial query. **Line Wrapping**: Auto-detects terminal width. Override with `--wrap always` or `--wrap never`. **Keyboard Shortcuts**: - `Ctrl+R`: Search command history (case-insensitive) - `Ctrl+C`: Cancel current operation or exit - `Up/Down`: Navigate command history **Logging**: Chat sessions write logs to platform-specific locations: - **macOS**: `$TMPDIR/kiro-log/kiro-chat.log` - **Linux**: `$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/kiro-log/kiro-chat.log` - **Windows**: `%TEMP%/kiro-log/logs/kiro-chat.log` Override with `KIRO_CHAT_LOG_FILE` environment variable: ```bash KIRO_CHAT_LOG_FILE=/tmp/my-debug.log kiro-cli chat ``` Disable ANSI colors in log output with `KIRO_LOG_NO_COLOR`: ```bash KIRO_LOG_NO_COLOR=1 kiro-cli chat ```

YOU MUST ONLY provide information that is explicitly documented in the sections above. If specific details about any tool, feature, or command are not documented, you MUST clearly state that the information is not available in the documentation. DO NOT generate plausible-sounding information or make assumptions about undocumented features.

--- Documentation for: save load chat session commands ---
The following documentation is provided inline. DO NOT attempt to read files - all content is below.



--- Document 1 ---
--- doc_meta: validated: 2026-04-09 commit: 4ae084db status: validated testable_headless: false category: slash_command title: /chat load description: Load previously saved conversation from file to resume session keywords: [chat, load, import, restore, resume, session, zip, json] related: [chat-save, chat-new] --- # /chat load Load previously saved conversation from file to resume session. ## Overview The `/chat load` command imports a conversation from a saved session file. Supports multiple file formats including the native Kiro export format, zip archives, and legacy V1 sessions. ## Usage ``` /chat load <path> ``` ## Parameters | Parameter | Required | Description | |-----------|----------|-------------| | `path` | Yes | Path to saved session file (.json or .zip) | ## Supported Formats | Format | Description | |--------|-------------| | Kiro | `kiro-session-export-v1` JSON (created by `/chat save`) | | Zip | Archive containing `session_metadata.json` + `conversation_log.jsonl` | | Legacy | V1 `ConversationState` JSON from older CLI versions | | SessionDataOnly | Bare `SessionData` JSON with optional companion `.jsonl` file | ## How It Works 1. Reads the file and auto-detects format 2. Extracts session metadata and conversation log 3. Creates a new session with a fresh UUID 4. Sets `imported_from` field to track origin The loaded session uses your current: - Agent configuration - Model configuration - MCP connections - Hooks ## Examples ### Example 1: Load from JSON File ``` /chat load my-session.json ``` **Output**: ``` Loaded session from /home/user/my-session.json ``` ### Example 2: Load from Zip Archive ``` /chat load backup.zip ``` ### Example 3: Load with Extension Fallback ``` /chat load backup ``` Tries `backup` first, then `backup.zip`, then `backup.json` if not found. ### Example 4: Load from Absolute Path ``` /chat load ~/backups/important-conversation.json ``` ## Troubleshooting ### Issue: File Not Found **Symptom**: "Failed to read" error **Cause**: File doesn't exist at path **Solution**: Check path. Command tries `.zip` then `.json` extensions automatically. ### Issue: Unrecognized Format **Symptom**: "Unrecognized session file format" error **Cause**: File is not a valid session export **Solution**: Ensure file was created with `/chat save` or is a valid legacy export. ### Issue: Invalid Zip Archive **Symptom**: "File has .zip extension but is not a valid zip archive" **Cause**: Corrupted or invalid zip file **Solution**: Re-export the session or use JSON format. ## Related Features - [/chat save](chat-save.md) - Save conversation to file - [/chat new](chat-new.md) - Start a new conversation - [kiro-cli chat --resume](../commands/chat.md) - Resume last conversation ## Technical Details **Extension Handling**: If path has no extension and file not found, tries `.zip` first, then `.json`. **Session ID**: Loaded sessions get a new UUID. The original path is stored in `imported_from`. **Format Detection**: Uses file extension for `.zip`, then probes JSON structure for other formats.

--- Document 2 ---
--- doc_meta: validated: 2026-04-09 commit: 4ae084db status: validated testable_headless: false category: slash_command title: /chat save description: Save current conversation to file for later resumption keywords: [chat, save, export, persist, session] related: [chat-load, chat-new] --- # /chat save Save current conversation to file for later resumption. ## Overview The `/chat save` command exports the current conversation state to a JSON file. Conversations are automatically saved to the database per-directory, but this command allows explicit file exports for backup, sharing, or custom storage. ## Usage ``` /chat save <path> /chat save <path> --force ``` ## Parameters | Parameter | Required | Description | |-----------|----------|-------------| | `path` | Yes | File path where conversation will be saved | | `--force`, `-f` | No | Overwrite existing file without prompting | ## How It Works Exports current conversation state as `kiro-session-export-v1` JSON including: - Session metadata (session ID, working directory, timestamps, title) - Complete log entries (message history) File can be loaded later with `/chat load` to restore the conversation. ## Examples ### Example 1: Save to File ``` /chat save my-session.json ``` **Output**: ``` Saved session to my-session.json ``` ### Example 2: Save Without Extension If no extension is provided, `.json` is automatically added: ``` /chat save my-session ``` Saves to `my-session.json`. ### Example 3: Overwrite Existing File ``` /chat save backup.json --force ``` ### Example 4: Save with Path ``` /chat save ~/backups/important-conversation.json ``` ## Troubleshooting ### Issue: File Already Exists **Symptom**: Error "File already exists" **Cause**: File at path already exists and --force not used **Solution**: Use `--force` flag or choose different path ### Issue: Permission Denied **Symptom**: "Failed to write" error **Cause**: No write permission for path **Solution**: Check directory permissions or use different path ### Issue: Invalid Path **Symptom**: Export fails **Cause**: Parent directory doesn't exist **Solution**: Create parent directories first or use existing path ## Related Features - [/chat load](chat-load.md) - Load saved conversation - [/chat new](chat-new.md) - Start fresh conversation - [kiro-cli chat](../commands/chat.md) - CLI session management ## Technical Details **File Format**: `kiro-session-export-v1` JSON envelope **Structure**: ```json { "format": "kiro-session-export-v1", "metadata": { "session_id": "...", "cwd": "/path/to/dir", "created_at": "2026-01-01T00:00:00Z", "updated_at": "2026-01-01T00:00:00Z", "title": "Session Title", "session_state": "..." }, "log_entries": [...] } ``` **State Preserved**: - Session ID - Working directory (cwd) - Created/updated timestamps - Session title - Log entries (complete message history) **State Not Preserved**: - Tool manager instance (recreated on load) - MCP connections (reconnected on load) - Agent configuration (uses current session's config) **Auto-Extension**: `.json` extension added automatically if no extension provided.

--- Document 3 ---
--- doc_meta: validated: 2026-04-09 commit: 4ae084db status: validated testable_headless: false category: feature title: Session Management description: Automatic session saving, resumption, and file-based storage keywords: [session, save, load, resume, auto-save, storage] related: [chat-save, chat-load, chat] --- # Session Management Automatic session saving, resumption, and file-based storage. ## Overview Kiro CLI automatically saves all chat sessions on every conversation turn. Sessions are stored per-directory as files. Resume from any previous session or export to portable files. ## Auto-Save **Automatic**: Every conversation turn saved **Scope**: Per-directory (each project has own sessions) **Storage**: `~/.kiro/sessions/cli/` - `{session_id}.json` - metadata (cwd, timestamps, session state) - `{session_id}.jsonl` - append-only conversation log - `{session_id}.lock` - lock file (exists only when session is active) **Session ID**: UUID for each session ## Managing Sessions ### From Command Line ```bash # Resume most recent session kiro-cli chat --resume # Interactive picker kiro-cli chat --resume-picker # List all sessions kiro-cli chat --list-sessions # Delete session kiro-cli chat --delete-session <SESSION_ID> ``` ### From Chat ```bash # Save to file /chat save <path> # Load from file /chat load <path> ``` **Note**: `.json` extension optional when loading. ## Session Storage **Files**: Sessions auto-saved per-directory to `~/.kiro/sessions/cli/` **Export**: Manual export via `/chat save` **Session ID**: UUID format (e.g., `f2946a26-3735-4b08-8d05-c928010302d5`) ## Examples ### Example 1: Resume Last Session ```bash kiro-cli chat --resume ``` Continues most recent conversation, restoring the model that was active when the session was saved. ### Example 2: Pick Session ```bash kiro-cli chat --resume-picker ``` Shows list of sessions to choose from. ### Example 3: Export to File ``` /chat save backup.json ``` Exports current session to file. ### Example 4: Save and Load Workflow ```bash # Save current session /chat save ./backup.json # Later, load it back /chat load ./backup.json ``` ## Troubleshooting ### Issue: No Sessions to Resume **Symptom**: "No saved chat sessions" **Cause**: No sessions in current directory **Solution**: Sessions are per-directory. Navigate to correct directory. ## Related - [/chat save](../slash-commands/chat-save.md) - Save command - [/chat load](../slash-commands/chat-load.md) - Load command - [kiro-cli chat](../commands/chat.md) - CLI options ## Limitations - Sessions stored per-directory - Auto-save to files only (not cloud) - Session IDs are UUIDs (not human-readable) - No cloud sync built-in - No session search by content ## Technical Details **Storage**: File-based in `~/.kiro/sessions/cli/` **Scope**: Sessions keyed by directory path **Auto-Save**: After every conversation turn **Model Preservation**: When resuming via `--resume`, the model active when the session was saved is restored. This includes models switched mid-session with `/model`. The `--model` CLI flag overrides the saved model.

The previous session is preserved and can be resumed later with `/chat`. MCP servers remain available (creates new server instances) ## Differences from /clear | Feature | `/chat new` | `/clear` | |---------|-------------|----------| | Creates new session ID | Yes | No | | Previous session resumable via `/chat` | Yes | No | | Clears conversation history | Yes | Yes | | Resets context window | Yes | Yes | | Preserves model selection | Yes | Yes | | Preserves agent selection | Yes | Yes | ## Related - `/chat` — List and load previous sessions - `/chat save` — Save conversation to file - `/chat load` — Load conversation from file - `/clear` — Clear conversation without creating new session ## Limitations - MCP servers are re-initialized in the new session (brief startup delay)

content.data:
Yes, both are available! Here's what you can do:

**Save this session:**
```
/chat save ~/john-project-session.json
```

**Resume next time** — either start Kiro from the project directory and run:
```
/chat load ~/john-project-session.json
```
Or use `kiro-cli chat --resume` to pick up the most recent session automatically (sessions are saved per-directory, so as long as you start Kiro from `/Users/billsneddon/Data/personal/Church/SS/John` it will remember).

The `--resume` flag is probably the easiest — just run `kiro-cli chat --resume` from the project directory and it'll restore the last conversation automatically.
can you show me the save command with the full project path?

```
/chat save /Users/billsneddon/Data/personal/Church/SS/John/kiro-session.json
```
